coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the thermostability, UV shielding and antimicrobial activity of transparent chitosan film by carbon quantum dots containing N/P
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lei
Liu, Xiaodong
Qi, Peng
Sun, Jun
Jiang, Shengling
Li, Hongfei
Gu, Xiaoyu
Zhang, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: The chitosan (CS) transparent film has attracted much attention in food and medicine packaging areas due to their biodegradability and good availability. A novel carbon quantum dots compound containing nitrogen and phosphorus (NP-CQDs) was obtained by reacting citric acids, with urea and phytic acids. The density of the film was increased, and the water vapor permeation was reduced by the presence of NP-CQDs. The introduction of 4 wt% NP-CQDs increased the water contact angle of the CS film from 79.2° to 105.8°. The shielding on UV-A and UV-B transmittance was increased with the NP-CQDs loading. The film containing 4 wt% NP-CQDs blocked more than 90.2% UV-A and 96.5% UV-B; however, it only blocked 26.8% visible light. It also exhibited better antibacterial activity to both E. coli and S. aureus than the control CS film. This work provided a feasible way to prepare multifunctional bio-safe film.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Carbon quantum dots
Thermal properties
Ultraviolet-blocking
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of chitosan film containing NP-CQDs
The density and water vapor permeation (WVP) of the CS/NP-CQDs films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21674008 and 51873005 ) and the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2018YFD1100403 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of NP-CQDs and CS/NP-CQDs films.
(a) FTIR (b) XRD (c) fluorescence emission and excitation (Inset: photograph of the NP-CQDs solution under UV light lamp), (d) TGA-DTG of NP-CQDs.
XPS spectra of NP-CQDs, (a) survey (b) C1s (c) N1s, and (d) P2p.
(a, b) The density and water vapor permeation (WVP) of the CS/NP-CQDs films; (c–f) Hydrogen bonding analysis of CS/NP-CQDs films.
(a, b) TGA and DTG curves of CS/NP-CQDs films.

(a) The heat release rate (HRR); (b) total heat release (THR) curves in MCC test; (c-e) The Raman spectra of CS/NP-CQDs films; (f) Damaged length and damaged area after burning; (g) Screenshots in vertical burning test.
(a) UV–vis transmittance curves; (b) Films under UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm.
The stress-strain tests result of CS/NP-CQDs films.
Antibacterial test for CS film and CS/NP-CQDs (96:4) film.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the thermostability, UV shielding and antimicrobial activity of transparent chitosan film by carbon quantum dots containing N/P

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The chitosan (CS) transparent film has attracted much attention in food and medicine packaging areas due to their biodegradability and good availability. A novel carbon quantum dots compound containing nitrogen and phosphorus (NP-CQDs) was obtained by reacting citric acids, with urea and phytic acids. The density of the film was increased, and the water vapor permeation was reduced by the presence of NP-CQDs. The introduction of 4 wt% NP-CQDs increased the water contact angle of the CS film from 79.2° to 105.8°. The shielding on UV-A and UV-B transmittance was increased with the NP-CQDs loading. The film containing 4 wt% NP-CQDs blocked more than 90.2% UV-A and 96.5% UV-B; however, it only blocked 26.8% visible light. It also exhibited better antibacterial activity to both E. coli and S. aureus than the control CS film. This work provided a feasible way to prepare multifunctional bio-safe film.

ce:section.ce:para: For a more sustainable environment, biogenetic and biodegradable materials are more desirable over petroleum-based non-degradable materials (Siracusa, Rocculi, Romani, & Dalla Rosa, 2008). Chitosan (CS) is a polysaccharide produced by the deacetylation of natural chitin, which is one of the most abundant natural polymers in the world, widely existed in the marine shellfish (Cheng & Cao, 2011; Mallakpour & Madani, 2015). Due to its safe biocompatibility and antibacterial properties, it has been accepted in medical areas such as pharmaceutical carriers, surgical sutures, dressing, and so on (Huang et al., 2021; Jawad & Nawi, 2012). Its abundant resource and transparent appearance have attracted many researchers to develop more applications of CS in the package and also other devices (Agarwal, Kóczán, Börcsök, Halász, & Pásztory, 2021; Kim et al., 2020; Osman et al., 2021). However, due to weak mechanical properties, it is difficult to fabricate into the films, bags and containers with satisfied strength and toughness (Kumar, Shukla, Baul, Mitra, & Halder, 2018; Zima, 2018). Considering the main application of CS products in medicine and food, the fillers should also be safe (Garavand et al., 2020), at the same time the air-tightness and antibacterial ability are also necessary; and for some outer packages utilization, thermostable, flame retardant, UV-resistant, and transparent CS films are ideal candidates (Cazón & Vázquez, 2019; Yang, Li, Niu, Wang, & Zhu, 2021; Zhou & Fu, 2020).

Some carbon-based particles including carbon black (Yang et al., 2015), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (Zhuang et al., 2020), and graphene (Razeghi & Pircheraghi, 2018) were added into thermoplastic polymers as strengthening fillers, and they also enhanced the thermostability of the matrix. It is also feasible for producing CS products (Buaki-Sogó, García-Carmona, Gil-Agustí, García-Pellicer, & Quijano-López, 2021; Han, Yan, Chen, & Li, 2011; Shen et al., 2014). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were accidentally discovered in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), which are the by-product in the preparation of CNTs. Therefore, the cost of CQDs is much less than that of CNTs. The CQDs, which have a diameter of less than 10 nm, exhibit excellent aqueous dispersibility and biocompatibility (Li, Li, & Zhang, 2017). More importantly, CQDs can be fabricated from natural plants as a carbon source (Nawaz, Zhang, Chen, You, & Xu, 2021). The CQDs derivatives can act as functional additives after introducing some organic groups or elements (Cuevas et al., 2019a; Karmakar, Das, Kundu, Maiti, & Saha, 2021).

In this work, a novel CQDs was fabricated by using citric acids as the carbonization precursor, which was loaded with urea as nitrogen (N) source and phytic acid as phosphorus (P) source. The obtained derivative of NP-CQDs was dispersed in the CS/acidic solution to cast film. The combustion behavior and thermal stability were comprehensively evaluated, and the mechanical property, UV-resistant and antibacterial of the CS/NP-CQDs films were investigated. To the best of our knowledge, to endow CS film with versatility by introducing carbon quantum dots containing N/P has been little reported so far.

Chitosan (CS, deacetylate degree >95%, viscosity: 100– 200 mPa·s) and phytic acid (PA) with a concentration of 50.0% were bought from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Glycerin (>99.0%), citric acid (>99.5%), and urea (>99.0%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD. The glacial acetic acid (>99%) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China).

At first, 1 g of citric acid, 0.4 g of urea, and 0.8 mL of the phytic acid aqueous solution was dissolved in 25 mL deionized water. After ultra-sonication for 20 min, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor. After heating at 180 °C for 8 h, the reactor was cooled down thoroughly and the reacted mixture was filtered off before freeze-drying. Finally, the final product was named as NP-CQDs (Rahimi-Aghdam, Shariatinia, Hakkarainen, & Haddadi-Asl, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chitosan film containing NP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CS/NP-CQDs films were prepared by solvent casting. 1 g mixture of CS/NP-CQDs at the weight ratio of 100:0, 99:1, 98:2, 97:3, 96:4, and 0.7 g of glycerin was dispersed in 100 mL of 1% (w/v) acetic acid by stirring under ambient for 12 h, the liquid was poured into a petri dish and dried at room temperature for 72 h to constant weight. Peeled off the film carefully (Priyadarshi, Kumar, & Negi, 2018). The chemical structure of NP-CQDs and the preparation process of CS/NP-CQDs films were schematically described in Fig. 1 .
On a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) Spectrometer (Nexus 670) of Nicolet Co., FTIR spectra were obtained under 64 scans in 650– 4000 cm−1.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Q50 apparatus from TA Instruments in USA. The thermal degradation was recorded in 30– 800 °C by a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. All samples were weighted at about 5 mg.

The heat resistance of CS/NP-CQD films was analyzed by using microscale combustion calorimetry (MCC). 4– 5 mg sample were heated from 25 to 650 °C at a rate of 1 °C/s under a 100 cc/min mixing flow of N2/O2 (80/20), the final result was averaged from at least five measurements (Wang et al., 2021).

Contact angles were determined using Data Physics OCA 20 contact angle system at ambient temperature. A 5 μL distilled water drop was used for each measurement. The results were averaged from at least eight different situations for each sample.

The morphology was observed under a scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI S4800) and a digital microscope (HIROXKH7700, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of (20 kV). For surface observation, the samples were sprayed with gold before the observation; for fracture observation, samples are broken using liquid nitrogen.

The elemental ratio and bonding status in NP-CQDs were detected by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) obtained from an ESCALAB250 (Thermo VG) spectrometer with an Al Ka excitation radiation (h
v 1486.6 eV). The XPSPEAK software was used for fitting.

X-ray diffraction patterns were scanned using a powder diffractometer Philips X'PERT (Netherlands) equipped with a monochromatic CuKα (1.5418 Å) X-ray source. The voltage used is 50 kV. The recorded range is in 2θ = 5– 60°, the step size is 0.02°.

The Water vapor permeation (WVP) of the films was evaluated following reported work (Priyadarshi, Sauraj, Kumar, & Negi, 2018). The samples were sealed upon a hollow sphere with a diameter of 16.20 mm containing 1 g CaCl2. The test container containing the film sample was weighed initially and then placed in a chamber at 25 °C and 75% relative humidity for 48 h. The container weight change was measured and recorded at every interval of 12 h.
(1)
WVP
g /
cm · s · Pa
=
w × d
A × t × p ×
R 1
−
R 2
where, w is the weight gained; t is the elapsed time; A is the exposed area of the film for transmission of water vapor; d is the thickness of the film, p is the saturation vapor pressure of water (3200 Pa at 25 °C), R
1 is the relative humidity in the desiccator outside the container, R
2 is the relative humidity inside the container.

UV–vis transmittance (250– 800 nm) of the film was measured using an Alpha1502 UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The UV-blocking property was intuitively depicted by the average transmittance of UV-A and UV-B, which are calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively. In addition, the visual appearance of the films is calculated using Eq. (4) (Yang, Gao, Zuo, Deng, & Dai, 2019). The excitation and emission spectra of CQDs are received by a spectrofluorometric (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan).
(2)
T
UV − A
=
∫ 315 400
T λ
d λ
/
∫ 315 400
d λ
(3)
T
UV − B
=
∫ 280 315
T λ
d λ
/
∫ 280 315
d λ
(4)
T Visible
=
∫ 400 760
T λ
d λ
/
∫ 400 760
d λ
where, “T
λ ” is the transmittance of the film at the wavelength of λ. T UV−A, T UV−B and T Visible are average transmittance in UV-A (315– 400 nm), UV-B (280– 315 nm), and visible light (400– 760 nm) region, respectively (Xu et al., 2021).

The vertical combustion behavior of CS/NP-CQDs films was tested according to the ASTM D4804-14 standard. The sample size was 150 × 50 × 0.15 mm3. The damaged length and burning time are recorded by a camera. The last results were averaged from 3– 5 parallel specimens.

An E. coli bacterial cell suspension at 1.0 × 108 CFU/mL was prepared after incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, 50 mg of CS/NP-CQDs films were dispersed in 10 mL of the cell suspension. The mixture was allowed to incubate at 37 °C for an additional 6 h, and then the mixture was diluted 1000 times, and 100 μL of the resulting diluted suspension was used to inoculate onto an LB agar plate. The inoculated cell culture was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h under ambient light conditions. S. aureus culture was also carried out using identical methodologies outlined above (Kováčová et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018).
The NP-CQDs were characterized by FTIR, XRD, TGA, and fluorescence propertiesanalysis. All the results were displayed in Fig. 2 .

The FTIR spectrum of NP-CQDs was shown in Fig. 2(a). The peaks at 3423 and 3270 cm−1 are ascribed to -OH or -NH groups, the peaks at 2922 and 1708 cm−1 correspond to CH and CO respectively (Priyadarshi, Kumar, & Negi, 2018). The peaks at 1654 and 1558 cm−1 are assigned to the CO and CC respectively. The characteristic peak of CN was observed at 1397 cm−1; besides, the peaks at 1131, 1056, and 785 cm−1 are ascribed to PO, PO, and P-O-H groups, respectively.

The XRD pattern of NP-CQDs was displayed in Fig. 2(b). There is a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ ≈ 22°, which corresponds to the diffraction from the graphitic carbon (002) plane (Huang et al., 2018).
The NP-CQDs exhibited photoluminescence. As shown in Fig. 2(c), at the excitation of 365 nm, the NP-CQDs showed the maximal emission strength at 450 nm.

Fig. 2(d) is the TGA and DTG curves of NP-CQDs, showing that the mass loss below 200 °C is attributed to the detachment and evaporation of adsorption and crystal water. The significant mass loss in 200– 500 °C can be assigned to the thermal degradation of NP-CQDs (Yang et al., 2018).

The elemental status of NP-CQDs was investigated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), main peaks appeared in the XPS survey spectra were assigned to ∼285 eV (C1s), ∼400 eV (N1s), ∼532 eV (O1s), ∼192 eV (P2s) and ∼133 eV (P2p) (Kalaiyarasan, Joseph, & Kumar, 2020). And the high-resolution scanning for C1s, N1s, and P2p was displayed in Fig. 3(b), (c) and (d) respectively.

In the XPS scanning of C1s, the peaks fitted at 284.6 eV, 285.2, 286.3, and 288.5 eV were attributed to the CC, CN, C-N/C-P, and O-C=O bonding respectively (Wang et al., 2020). In the scanning of N1s, the first peak at 400 eV is assigned to the N in the pyrrole group, and the other one at 401 eV is corresponding to the graphite N, documenting some N atoms were covalently bonded to the graphene skeleton (Cao, Hu, Chen, Xu, & Chen, 2017; Rahimi-Aghdam et al., 2020). Two peaks in the fitted spectra of P2p at 133.4 and 134.2 eV, represent the PO and PC and PN bond respectively (Rahimi-Aghdam et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The density and water vapor permeation (WVP) of the CS/NP-CQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b), with the increased loading of NP-CQDs, the density of the film became higher, meaning the film became more compact. It was speculated that the CS molecular chains were connected with the distributed NP-CQDs particles by H-bond attraction (Xu et al., 2020). Therefore, the free volume between CS molecular chains was reduced, as a result, the value of WVP is declined, meaning the air-tightness was enhanced.

The H-bonding attraction between CS and NP-CQDs was studied by FTIR. According to the FTIR spectra of films shown in Fig. 4(c) and (d), with the increase of loading of NP-CQDs, the wideband for the -OH in 3000– 3500 cm−1 was further widened, and the peak height above 3200 cm−1 grew up, which represents the hydrogen bond inter-attraction between CS molecules and NP-CQDs by the -OH and -NH groups (Lecaros et al., 2019).

Fig. 4(e) showed the contact angle of the CS/NP-CQDs films increased from 79.2° to 105.8° with the NP-CQDs loading increased from 1 to 4 wt%, meaning the CS/NP-CQDs films became more hydrophobic (Njuguna, Pielichowski, & Desai, 2008), the reason can be ascribed to more -OH groups in CS was connected with NP-CQDs by H-bonds, so the hydrophilia declined.

Fig. 4(f) reported XRD patterns of CS/NP-CQDs films. With the addition of glycerol to CS film, the chitosan plasticizes and becomes a hydrated polymorph, four main peaks around 8°, 11.2°, 17.6°, and 20.5° appeared in the CS film (Chen et al., 2018; Matet, Heuzey, Pollet, Ajji, & Avérous, 2013; Meng, Heuzey, & Carreau, 2014). The intensity of those diffraction peaks gradually attenuated and formed a larger amorphous character with the increase of NP-CQDs loading, indicating some regular arrangement of CS molecules was disturbed by NP-CQDs (Jawad, Nawi, Mohamed, & Wilson, 2017; L. Zhang, Zhang, Chen, Ma, & Xia, 2021).
The TGA and DTG curves of CS/NP-CQDs films under N2 are shown in Fig. 5 .

It was seen from Fig. 5 that there are two main decomposition stages, where the first stage in 150– 250 °C is related to the initial depolymerization of CS and decomposition of glycerol (Barbosa, Francisco, Ferreira, & Cavalheiro, 2019), the 5% mass loss temperature (T5%) moved ahead with the introduction of increasing NP-CQDs, the reason was ascribed to the earlier decomposition of CS-induced by the PA in NP-CQDs.

The second stage in 250– 500 °C is due to the decomposition of the main chain and pyranose rings of CS, resulting in the generation of H2O, CO, CO2, and some other fragments (Galvis-Sánchez, Sousa, Hilliou, Gonçalves, & Souza, 2016; Leistner, Abu-Odeh, Rohmer, & Grunlan, 2015). Based on the DTG curves, the decomposition of CS was postponed to a higher temperature by increased NP-CQDs adding. The results documented NP-CQDs protected the CS molecules from pyrolysis during heating.

At last, the CS film left about 27.9 wt% residue, whilst the CS film containing 1– 4 wt% NP-CQDs left more char than the calculated value. The TGA results documented that the NP-CQDs improved the thermostability of CS film especially at higher temperatures by catalysis on charring, more CS was kept in solid-phase instead of pyrolysis and volatilization (Khose, Pethsangave, Wadekar, Ray, & Some, 2018).
The heat release rate (HRR) and total heat release (THR) of the CS/NP-CQDs films in heating were investigated by MCC and illustrated in Fig. 6 (a) and (b).

Two peaks emerged in HRR curves around 200 and 310 °C in all the 3 samples, the TpHRR1 and pHRR1 were similar for the 3 samples, but the value of pHRR2 gradually decreased with the increased adding of NP-CQDs. It was 125.9 W/g for CS film, declined to 85.9 W/g and 74.7 W/g after adding 2 and 4 wt% NP-CQDs respectively. Accordingly, as shown in Fig. 6(b), the THR decreased by 15.2% and 32.6% respectively in comparison with that of CS film.

The Raman spectra of the CS/NP-CQDs films calcined at 600 °C for 5 min were shown in Fig. 6(c–e). In the 3 samples, Two peaks appeared in all the 3 samples, the peak at1363 cm−1 is ascribed to deformed C; and the peak at 1593 cm−1 is assigned to graphited C. the peak intensity ratio of the D to G peak was defined as ID/IG, lower value of ID/IG indicated a higher degree of graphitization (Wang et al., 2020). The value was 3.60 for the CS film, it went down to 3.38 and 2.85 with 2 wt% and 4 wt% NP-CQDs adding respectively, documented higher graphitization degree (Rahimi-Aghdam, Shariatinia, Hakkarainen, & Haddadi-Asl, 2019). In summary, during heating, NP-CQDs participate in the pyrolysis of CS and catalyze the carbonization, which acts as a protective layer to the substrate CS (Alongi, Ciobanu, & Malucelli, 2011; Li et al., 2019; Liu & Liang, 2017).

The vertical burning test results were summarized in Fig. 6(g), the CS film burnt rapidly within 10 s after contact with the fire, and continue burning after the fire was removed until it was burnt out at last. The whole process lasted for about 15 s.

For the CS films containing NP-CQDs, the films were difficult to be ignited, and they tended to self-extinguish after leaving the flame. We can see from Fig. 6(f) that the control CS films with the length of 15 cm burnt out after ignition, but the damaged length of the CS/4 wt% NP-CQDs was only 4.8 cm; similarly, the damaged area was also decrease from 75 cm2 to 12.5 cm2, in other words, more than 83.3% substrate was kept after ignition.

Fig. 7 displayed the UV–vis transmittance of the CS/NP-CQDs films. We can see that about 40.7% UV-A and 25.2% UV-B radiation penetrated the CS film respectively, whilst the UV was effectively blocked by increasing adding of NP-CQDs. When the loading of NP-CQDs reached 4 wt%, the UV-A and UV-B transmission were reduced to 9.8% and 3.5% respectively. In other words, 90.2% UV-A and 96.5% UV-B were blocked. It proved that the NP-CQDs are an excellent UV absorbent (Hess et al., 2017).

The transmittance of the visible light of the control CS film was 84.2%, so it looked transparent. The transmittance declined with the increase of NP-CQDs, but until the NP-CQDs loading went up to 4 wt%, T Visible still reached 73.2%; so the film still looked transparent.

Some CQDs particles exhibit special photoluminescence (Cuevas et al., 2019b), the photographs shown in Fig. 7(b) are the prepared films with a diameter of 3 cm under the UV light (10-W household fluorescent flashlight) at a wavelength of 365 nm (Nawi, Jawad, Sabar, & Ngah, 2011a, 2011b), with the increase NP-CQDs loading, the CS films became bright. The phenomenon reminded us the CS/NP-CQDs films can be developed for smart packaging.
The incorporation of NP-CQDs on the mechanical properties of the CS films was investigated by stress-strain tests, and the results were shown in Fig. 8 .

The tensile strength of the control CS film was 29.8 MPa, the elongation at break was 35.5% and Young's modulus was 8.9 MPa, with the increased loading of NP-CQDs from 1 to 2 and 3 wt%, the mechanical property was enhanced compared with the control sample. The tensile strength and Young's modulus of the film containing 2 wt% of NP-CQDs reached 44.7 MPa and 13.5 MPa, with 38.6% and 52.4% extension, respectively. The enhancement of mechanical properties was ascribed to the hydrogen bonding between NP-CQDs and CS molecules (Qin et al., 2021; Sui, Li, & Yang, 2020). Continue to increase the NP-CQDs loading to 4 wt% the tensile strength and elasticity went down; the reason was caused by the heavy aggregation of nano-sized particles in the CS phase.
The antibacterial activity of the CS/NP-CQDs films was evaluated using E. coli and S. aureus, and the results were shown in Fig. 9 .

It can be seen in Fig. 9 that the CS/NP-CQDs films showed better inhibition of E. coli and S. aureus in comparison with the CS films. This is likely because that NP-CQDs is an effective photosensitizer, that generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as O2 •−, O2, HO2 − under light irradiation (Chong et al., 2016; Kováčová et al., 2018; Travlou et al., 2018) and the ROS-mediated oxidative stress plays an important role in the antibacterial mechanism in which cell lipids and proteins are attacked by the ROS, causing oxidative stress on the cell film, and thus result in cell film destructions (Su et al., 2021).

A novel nitrogen‑phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (NP-CQDs) was fabricated by hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid, phytic acid, and urea. The chitosan film containing NP-CQDs was prepared through solution casting. The CS/NP-CQDs films exhibited enhanced thermal stability and fire resistance. The well-dispersed NP-CQDs connected with the CS chains by H-bond attraction, which enhanced the airtightness and strength of the film. The tensile strength and Young's modulus of the CS film were 29.8 MPa and 8.9 MPa respectively, however, the presence of 2 wt% NP-CQDs increased the tensile strength and Young's modulus to 44.7 MPa and 13.5 MPa respectively. The total UV shielding from transparent CS/NP-CQDs (96:4) film was above 90%. The CS/NP-CQDs film also showed antibacterial properties, it was effective for inhibition of either E. coli or S. aureus. The methodology developed in this work has great potential in producing commercialized thermalstable, UV resistant, and antibacterial films.

Lei Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaodong Liu: Data curation, Resources. Peng Qi: Resources. Jun Sun: Methodology. Shengling Jiang: Software, Validation. Hongfei Li: Software, Validation. Xiaoyu Gu: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Sheng Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21674008 and 51873005) and the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2018YFD1100403).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2021.118957.
